content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 21 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 110r–111v | **Source Images:** DEU_021_page_001.jpg, DEU_021_page_002.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), p. 156 | **LXX Image:** DEU_021_vaticanus_page_001.jpg

---

**1** If in the land that the LORD your God is giving you to possess, a slain person is found fallen in the open field, and it is not known who struck him down—

**2** then your elders and your judges shall go out, and they shall measure [the distance] to the cities that are around the slain person.

**3** And it shall be that the city nearest to the slain person—the elders of that city shall take a heifer [eglah] of the herd, one that has not been worked and has not pulled in a yoke.

**4** And the elders of that city shall bring the heifer down to a wadi with running water, to a place that is not plowed and not sown, and they shall break the neck of the heifer there in the wadi.

**5** And the priests, the sons of Levi, shall draw near—for them the LORD your God has chosen to minister to him and to bless in the name of the LORD, and by their word every dispute and every assault shall be decided.

**6** And all the elders of that city, the nearest to the slain man, shall wash their hands over the heifer whose neck was broken in the wadi.

**7** And they shall answer and say: Our hands did not shed this blood, and our eyes did not see [it].

**8** Atone, O LORD, for your people Yisra'el, whom you have redeemed, and do not set [the guilt of] innocent blood in the midst of your people Yisra'el. And the blood-guilt shall be atoned for them.

**9** And you shall purge the [guilt of] innocent blood from your midst, for you do what is right in the eyes of the LORD.

**10** When you go out to battle against your enemies, and the LORD your God gives them into your hand, and you take them captive,

**11** and you see among the captives a woman of beautiful form [eshet yefat to'ar], and you desire her and would take her as a wife for yourself—

**12** then you shall bring her into the midst of your house, and she shall shave her head and do her nails,

**13** and she shall take off the garment of her captivity from upon her, and she shall remain in your house and weep for her father and her mother a full month; and after that you may go in to her and be her husband, and she shall be your wife.

**14** And it shall be, if you find no delight in her, then you shall let her go wherever she wishes; but you shall certainly not sell her for silver. You shall not treat her as a slave, because you have humbled her.

**15** If a man has two wives, the one loved and the other unloved, and they have borne him sons—the loved and the unloved—and the firstborn son belongs to the unloved one,

**16** then on the day he causes his sons to inherit what he has, he is not permitted to treat the son of the loved as the firstborn before the son of the unloved, who is the firstborn.

**17** Rather, the firstborn—the son of the unloved—he shall acknowledge, to give him a double portion of all that he has; for he is the first of his vigor, the right of the firstborn is his.

**18** If a man has a stubborn and rebellious son [ben sorer u-moreh], who does not obey the voice of his father or the voice of his mother, and they have disciplined him but he does not listen to them—

**19** then his father and his mother shall take hold of him and bring him out to the elders of his city and to the gate of his place.

**20** And they shall say to the elders of his city: This son of ours is stubborn and rebellious; he does not obey our voice—he is a glutton and a drunkard.

**21** And all the men of his city shall stone him with stones, and he shall die. So you shall purge the evil from your midst, and all Yisra'el shall hear, and fear.

**22** And if a man has committed a sin [worthy of] a death sentence, and he is put to death, and you hang him on a tree—

**23** his body shall not remain overnight on the tree; rather you shall surely bury him on that day, for a hanged man is a curse of God [qilelat Elohim], and you shall not defile your land that the LORD your God is giving you as an inheritance.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**v.1–9 "Eglah arufah"** — The rite of the broken-necked heifer (*eglah arufah*) is unique to Deuteronomy and has no parallel in the ancient Near East. The wadi with running water (*nachal eitan*, lit. "perennial wadi") is rendered "wadi with running water" to capture the legal specificity. EMET preserves "heifer" rather than "calf" because the MT *eglah* indicates a young female bovine that has not yet been put to work.

**v.4 "break the neck"** — Hebrew *araf* (Pi'el), a specialized verb occurring only in this context and Exod 13:13 (firstborn donkey). It denotes snapping the cervical vertebrae, not slaughter. EMET renders literally.

**v.8 "Atone"** — Hebrew *kapper*, the same root as *Yom Kippur*. The imperative addressed to YHWH is unusual and bold: the community collectively appeals for divine expiation. EMET retains "atone" as the most literal equivalent.

**v.11 "eshet yefat to'ar"** — Lit. "a woman of beautiful form." EMET preserves the full phrase rather than condensing to "a beautiful captive woman," because *to'ar* (form/appearance) is the specific term.

**v.12 "do her nails"** — Hebrew *asetha et tsipporneha*, lit. "she shall do/make her nails." The exact practice (cut? pare? grow out?) is debated; EMET renders literally without resolving the ambiguity.

**v.17 "first of his vigor"** — Hebrew *reshit ono*, lit. "beginning of his strength/vigor." A formulaic expression (cf. Gen 49:3). EMET preserves it over euphemistic renderings.

**v.22–23 "qilelat Elohim"** — Lit. "a curse of God" or "a hanging by/of God." The genitive is ambiguous: it may mean (a) one cursed by God, (b) an insult/dishonor to God, or (c) a hanging that belongs to the divine domain. Paul cites this verse in Gal 3:13. EMET renders literally as "curse of God" and preserves the ambiguity.

**LXX comparison** — Vaticanus (p. 156) on vv. 22–23 reads *kekataramenos hypo Theou* ("cursed by God"), choosing interpretation (a). MT is ambiguous. This is a significant divergence in theological import; the LXX resolves the ambiguity toward the passive sense.

*— EMET*
